claim: Made me feel closer to my classmate One-sample t-test Effect size: d = 5.65 p < .001
note: Ali's Phase 2: reported 'd = 5.65 p < .001', reproduced 'd(av) = 5.64, p < .001.' -> 'Not reproducible'. HELD per the project scope rule, but the exclusion is effect-size-only: t(38) = 25.32, p, and all Ms/SDs reproduce EXACTLY; d_av = 5.6446 vs reported 5.65, an absolute difference of 0.0054 that rounds to 5.64. VERIFIED across all 7 rows of Supplementary Table 1 (values recovered from the article + Ali's SPSS .spv): all 7 t values reproduce exactly; d_av reproduces 3 of 7 published d exactly while dz reproduces only the trivial 0.00; no single formula reproduces the whole table, which is itself off by 0.0316 on the 'Belonging' row and carries two wrong descriptives. Ali used d_av (the authors' method), not SPSS's dz. Wave-1 Bayes factors use t/df/n and never d, so this claim is statistically usable if the scope rule is refined to 'reproducible on the test statistic and p-value'.
scope_exclusion_reason: Out of scope: Almuhanna Phase 2 status is 'Not reproducible', not 'Fully reproducible'.
model_formula: paired t-test: post - pre; average-SD Cohen's d
contrast_direction: post minus pre
extraction_note: Paired-samples t test, POST - PRE, pairwise-complete. Effect size is the manuscript's average-SD d = mean_diff/((sd_pre+sd_post)/2) = 5.6446 vs reported 5.65 (absolute difference 0.0054). SPSS's difference-score d (dz) = 4.055 is NOT what the article reports. t = 25.322915 reproduces the reported t = 25.32 exactly. ALI (Phase 2): reproduced 'd(av) = 5.64, p < .001.' -> 'Not reproducible'. Ali used the same average-SD method, so his verdict on this result turns only on rounding at the second decimal of d, not on the test statistic. Validated across all 7 rows of Supplementary Table 1: all 7 t values reproduce exactly, d_av reproduces 3 of 7 published d values exactly (dz reproduces 1, the trivial 0.00), and no single formula reproduces the whole table. Raw .sav holds 77 rows; 39 have complete pre/post on this item.

claim: Made me feel closer to my classmate One-sample t-test Effect size: d = 5.65 p < .001

NOTE the error here is essentially the same size as the key result's 0.0054 -- the split verdict tracks a rounding boundary, not a difference in reproducibility. claim: We found that answering questions about texts (retrieval practice) that were read with and without multitasking, both led to better recall 7 days later than texts read with multitasking followed by rereading..
note: RESOLVED: the article's F(2,116) = 4.22, p < .02, eta_p2 = .07 IS reproducible. The earlier mismatch (F = 10.43) was a sums-of-squares issue diagnosed by Peter Allen: the authors used SPSS/SAS (Type III SS) while the script used weighted Type I/II marginals, which diverge because Sex is unbalanced (45 F, 15 M). Type III gives F = 4.223231, p = .016966, eta_p2 = .067872 -- matches the article. Moved from held to pending: this is a mixed ANOVA with a 2-df numerator, so it is Wave 2 (needs a raw-data Bayesian model), NOT reducible to a Wave-1 t-equivalent Bayes factor.
model_formula: sex-controlled repeated-measures GLM: delayed_recall_percent ~ condition + condition:Sex error term; condition order = RR, MMRP, RP
contrast_direction: delayed recall differs across multitask + reread, multitask + retrieval practice, and no multitask + retrieval practice
extraction_note: The article's Hypothesis 2 delayed-recall condition means are reproduced from the uploaded author data: RR M = 22.02381 (SD = 22.329397), MMRP M = 35.238095 (SD = 23.075031), RP M = 38.333333 (SD = 24.916556). Using all complete cases and controlling for Sex gives F(2, 116) = 4.223231, p = 0.0169658, eta_p2 = 0.067872. The unadjusted author-syntax-style repeated-measures GLM gives F(2, 118) = 10.146464, p = 8.58697e-05, eta_p2 = 0.146739. Sums of squares: TYPE III (SPSS/SAS default), using unweighted marginal means and an effective n of g * harmonic-mean(group sizes) = 45. This recovers the published F(2, 116) = 4.22, eta_p2 = .07. The earlier reported mismatch (F = 10.427106) came from weighted Type I/II marginals, which are inflated here because the between factor is unbalanced (Sex: 45 F, 15 M). Diagnosis credited to Peter Allen; cross-checked against afex::aov_ez(type = 3). The script does not exclude participant 39 and does not force the article value. Variable ordering only changes labels/contrasts, not the omnibus condition F.

claim: We found a testing effect for practiced content, whereas no transfer effect occurred for untested content from the same lecture sessions.
note: RESOLVED. Almuhanna's KEY target is F(1,64) = 5.33, p = .024, eta_p2 = .08, marked 'Fully reproducible' -- the follow-up testing effect for REVIEWED content, not the learning-condition x question-type interaction (F = 5.18) that the reproduction script previously saved as its only row. Our recomputation gives F(1,64) = 5.325433, p = .0242607, eta_p2 = .076818, matching the article exactly (Glaser & Richter, 2023). The value was always computed but was recorded only inside the extraction_note; study_43.R now saves it as its own row (id 52). Model matches the authors' own Step-1 SPSS GLM syntax with Type III SS. Wave 2: follow-up simple effect inside a 2x2x2 mixed ANOVA whose error term comes from the full GLM, so it is NOT a Wave-1 t-equivalent despite the 1-df numerator.
project_verified_result: Testing effect for reviewed content: F(1, 64) = 5.33, p = .024, eta_p2 = .08
raw_variable_names: Crit_Score_Testing_old; Crit_Score_Testing_New; Crit_Score_Restudy_old; Crit_Score_Restudy_New; Lecture; questiontype_crit
model_formula: SPSS GLM Crit_Score_Testing_old Crit_Score_Testing_New Crit_Score_Restudy_old Crit_Score_Restudy_New BY Lecture questiontype_crit /WSFACTOR = Learning_condition 2 Polynomial Old_vs_New 2 Polynomial /WSDESIGN = Learning_condition Old_vs_New Learning_condition*Old_vs_New /DESIGN = Lecture questiontype_crit
contrast_direction: Final-test performance on questions referring to TESTED content exceeds performance on questions referring to RESTUDIED content, within reviewed content.
extraction_note: ROW: follow-up simple effect for REVIEWED content -- this is Almuhanna's KEY target for study_43 (reported F(1,64) = 5.33, p = .024, eta_p2 = .08; marked Fully reproducible). Article: Glaser & Richter (2023), 'Follow-up tests revealed a testing effect for questions referring to reviewed content.' Recomputed from the author's SPSS GLM target using complete cases on the four criterion-score variables. The target within-subject interaction was reconstructed as the contrast (Testing_old - Testing_New) - (Restudy_old - Restudy_New), then tested as the intercept in lm(contrast ~ Lecture + questiontype_crit) using sum contrasts, matching the SPSS repeated-measures GLM with Lecture and questiontype_crit as between-subject factors and no Lecture x questiontype_crit term. n_total = 67; Lecture counts: 1=39; 2=28; questiontype_crit counts: -1=37; 1=30. Target interaction: F(1, 64) = 5.183127, p = 0.0261602, eta_p2 = 0.074919. Follow-up reviewed/tested content contrast: F(1, 64) = 5.325433, p = 0.0242607, eta_p2 = 0.076818. Follow-up unreviewed-content contrast: F(1, 64) = 0.739777, p = 0.392943, eta_p2 = 0.011427. Question-type main effect: F(1, 64) = 19.091631, p = 4.66226e-05, eta_p2 = 0.229766. Learning-condition main effect: F(1, 64) = 0.634395, p = 0.428692, eta_p2 = 0.009815.

claim: Taking a multiple-choice test led to predictions about future performance that were 10% higher than those taking a short-answer test.
note: MOVED TO WAVE 1. The between-subjects Condition effect F(1,119) is exactly an equal-variance independent-samples t-test on each subject's mean of the three prediction scores: t(119)=2.232190, F=t^2=4.982671, p=.027474 two-sided, matching the article. df 119 = 121-2 (63 vs 58). Meets the Wave-1 rule: two independent groups, single per-subject score, pooled (Student, not Welch) error, F=t^2. Sidedness follows the published non-directional F, so two-sided. Reclassified from mixed_anova to independent_t_test in the recomputed row. NOTE the DV is a composite (average of remember/MC/OE), which is the quantity the between-subjects F itself tests. Supersedes the earlier 'candidate, pending review' note.
project_verified_result: Between-subjects Condition effect: F(1,119)=4.98 = t(119)^2; t(119)=2.23, p=.027 (equal-variance independent t on each subject's mean of the three prediction scores)
model_formula: SPSS GLM PercentRember PercentCorrectMC PercentCorrectOE BY Condition /WSFACTOR=factor1 3 Polynomial /WSDESIGN=factor1 /DESIGN=Condition
contrast_direction: between-subjects main effect of testing condition on average predicted future performance
extraction_note: RECLASSIFIED to Wave 1. The SPSS mixed GLM 'Tests of Between-Subjects Effects' Condition effect collapses each subject's three within-measures (PercentRember, PercentCorrectMC, PercentCorrectOE) to their mean and does a one-way between-groups ANOVA on those means -- i.e. an equal-variance independent-samples t-test of Condition 1 (n=63) vs Condition 2 (n=58). Recomputed via lm(row_mean ~ Condition): t(119) = 2.232190, F(1,119) = 4.982671, p = .027474 (two-sided), Cohen's d(pooled) = 0.406200; matches the published F(1,119)=4.98, p=.027, eta_p2=.04. df 119 = 121 - 2 confirms the two-group independent-t equivalence. Sidedness is two-sided because the published test was a non-directional F. The composite DV (mean of three percent-correct measures) is exactly the quantity the between-subjects F tests.

Omega squared is computed from the ordinary one-way ANOVA sums of squares, matching the article's reported omega2. Group summaries: one: n=38, M=7.447368, SD=1.703690; two: n=40, M=6.575000, SD=1.795828; three: n=38, M=7.105263, SD=2.251600; mastery: n=38, M=9.473684, SD=1.006381. Reported BF10 = 2.06e8 is retained as article metadata but is not recomputed here.
